1: ‘After these faithful acts (ʾaḥărê haddĕḇārîm wĕhāʾĕmet hāʾēlleh), Sennacherib king of Assyria came.’ The Chronicler’s opening formula is deliberate: ʾaḥărê (after) + the list of Hezekiah’s temple reforms in 2 Chronicles 29–31 signals that the trial follows the faithfulness, not precedes it. hāʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness) as an abstract noun qualifying ‘these acts’ is unusual — the phrase essentially means ‘these acts of faithfulness.’ The Chronicler’s theology of testing: YHWH permits the Assyrian threat precisely as a sequel to a period of covenant fidelity, not as punishment for sin.
2: wayyarʾ ḥizkîyāhû kî bāʾ — ‘Hezekiah saw that Sennacherib had come’ (rāʾāh + infinitive construct: he saw that Sennacherib had come, i.e., his coming was a visible fait accompli). ûpānāyw lamillḥāmāh ʿal-yĕrûšālāim — ‘and his face was toward war against Jerusalem.’ The expression pānāyw ʿal (his face toward) is the directional-intention idiom: the military threat is resolved and directed, not ambiguous. Hezekiah’s response is immediate strategic action rather than panic.
3: wayyiwwāʿaṣ ʿim-śārāyw — ‘he took counsel with his officers.’ The Niphal of yāʿaṣ (to counsel, advise) in this reflexive sense means ‘he consulted together with.’ The subject of the counsel is laʾsēṭōm ʾet-mêyê hammayĕnôt = to stop the waters of the springs outside the city. The practical military logic: deny the invading army its water supply while securing Jerusalem’s own. The ‘mighty men’ (gibbōrêhā) are the technical term for the standing military force, not a general word for ‘people.’
4: wayyiqqāhᵃlû ʿam-rāb — ‘a large assembly gathered.’ qāhal (assembly, congregation) carries the covenant-assembly overtone of Sinai; its use here for a military/civic muster gives the water-blocking operation a quasi-communal significance. The purpose: lāsĕtōm ʾet-kol-hammayĕnôt = to stop all the springs (maʿyānôt + naḥal = springs and brooks/streams). The naḥal (seasonal watercourse) running through the land is specifically identified as the water they wished to deny the Assyrian kings, framing it as a denial-of-resources strategy.
5: wayyithazzēq — ‘he strengthened himself’ (Hithpael of ḥāzaq, reflexive). The Hithpael indicates Hezekiah’s active self-strengthening: he built up the wall (wayyiḇen ʾet-kol-hahōmāh) that was broken down, raised towers (hammigdālôt), built another wall outside, reinforced the Millo (wayyḥazzēq ʾet-hammillōʾ), and made weapons and shields. The catalog of five building/armament actions shows systematic defense preparation. millōʾ (fill, the Millo) = the stone terrace/fill structure supporting the Davidic city from the time of David (2 Samuel 5:9).
6: wayyittēn śārê milḥāmôt ʿal-hāʿām — ‘he appointed officers of war over the people.’ The verb nātan (to give, appoint) in the sense of commissioning/setting over is the appointment idiom. Hezekiah then gathered them at the broad place at the city gate (bīrᵉḥôḇ šaʿar hāʿîr) and spoke to their heart (wayyḏabbēr ʿal-libbām) = addressed them encouragingly. The ʿal-lēḇ idiom (speak to the heart) is intimate and reassuring speech — used by Joseph comforting his brothers (Genesis 50:21), Boaz with Ruth (Ruth 2:13), and the suffering servant in Isaiah 40:2.
7: ḥizqû wĕʾimṣû — ‘Be strong and courageous!’ The combined imperative formula is the classic Deuteronomic charge: YHWH uses this exact phrase when commissioning Joshua (Joshua 1:6, 7, 9) and David charges Solomon with it (1 Chronicles 22:13; 2 Chronicles 28:20). ʾāmaṣ (Qal imperative: be courageous, firm) and ḥāzaq (be strong) form a fixed pair. ʾal-tîrĕʾû wĕʾal-tēḥattû = ‘do not fear and do not be dismayed/shattered’ — ḥātat (Niphal: to be shattered, broken, terrified) is a stronger term than general fear; it denotes being broken by terror. The charge draws on the conquest and temple-building traditions simultaneously.
8: ʿimmô zĕrôaʿ bāśār wĕʿimmānû YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — ‘With him is an arm of flesh, but with us is YHWH our God.’ The antithesis bāśār (flesh) vs. YHWH ʾĕlōhênû (YHWH our God) is one of the sharpest formulations of the Chronicler’s theology. zĕrôaʿ bāśār (arm of flesh) echoes the Jeremiah/Isaiah idiom: Jeremiah 17:5 curses the one who ‘makes flesh his arm (śām bāśār zĕrōʿô).’ The Chronicler’s speech has Hezekiah anticipate prophetic vocabulary, framing the Assyrian military force as categorically weak. wayyissāmᵉḵû hāʿām = ‘the people took courage/leaned on the words.’ sāmaḵ (to lean, support, uphold) appears in Psalms 3:5; 2 Chronicles 37:24; 54:4 as a trust-verb.
9: Sennacherib sends his servants to Jerusalem while he was at Lachish (wĕhûʾ ʿal-lāḵîš). The geographic specificity is important: Lachish was the greatest military siege operation of the period (confirmed by the Lachish reliefs at Nineveh and the Lachish letters). Hezekiah’s refusal to capitulate forces Sennacherib to operate against Jerusalem by proxy while still besieging Lachish. The servants carry a message (ʾal-yĕhûḏāh wĕʿal-kol-yôšĕḇê yĕrûšālayim) to Judah and to all inhabitants of Jerusalem.
10: ʿal-meh ʾattem bōṭĕḥîm — ‘On what are you relying?’ The bāṭaḥ root (to trust, rely, be confident) is the key trust-vocabulary of the Psalms (Psalms 4:5; 2 Chronicles 9:10; 2 Chronicles 21:7). Sennacherib’s taunt deploys the language of faith against itself: he mocks Judah’s ‘confidence’ by asserting it is misplaced. The question is phrased as an aggressive interrogative: ʿal-meh (on what?) implies ‘there is nothing.’
11: hălōʾ ḥizkîyāhû massît ʾetḵem — ‘Is not Hezekiah misleading you?’ The Hiphil sût (to entice, mislead) is the same word used for satanic incitement (1 Chronicles 21:1) and Ahab’s false prophets (2 Chronicles 18:2). Sennacherib frames Hezekiah as a deceiver. The argument: ‘YHWH our God will deliver us from the hand of the king of Assyria’ — if Hezekiah says this, Sennacherib implies, he is lying. The taunt assumes YHWH cannot deliver, which the Chronicler will refute by narrative outcome.
12: hălōʾ-hûʾ ḥizkîyāhû hēsîr ʾet-bāmōtāyw wĕʾet-mizbĕḥōtāyw — ‘Has not Hezekiah himself removed his high places and altars?’ Sennacherib deploys Hezekiah’s reform against him: the very centralization of worship at Jerusalem that the Chronicler presents as faithfulness, the Assyrian reads as having offended the Judahite deity by eliminating the regional shrines. The argument rests on a misunderstanding (or deliberate misrepresentation) of Yahwistic worship, but it is rhetorically sophisticated: Hezekiah has reduced YHWH’s cultic footprint; maybe YHWH is now weak or angry.
13: hᵃlōʾ tēḏĕʿû meh ʿāśîtî ʾănî wăʾăḇôtay lĕkol-ʿammê hāʾărāṣôt — ‘Do you not know what I and my fathers have done to all the peoples of the lands?’ The historical-precedent argument: Sennacherib appeals to the documented track record of Assyrian conquest. ʿāśāh (to do) applied to military conquest summarizes the fall of nations. The rhetorical question form (hᵃlōʾ) expects the answer ‘yes, you know’ — Assyrian military reputation was used deliberately as a weapon of psychological terror.
14: mî bĕkol-ʾĕlōhê haggôyim hāʾēlleh ʾăšer-hēḥĕrîmû ʾăḇôtay — ‘Who among all the gods of those nations that my fathers devoted to destruction...?’ ḥāram (Hiphil: to devote to destruction/ban) is the holy-war vocabulary — the same term for Israel’s warfare against Canaan (Deuteronomy 7:2; Joshua 6:17). Sennacherib ironically uses Israel’s own warfare terminology to describe Assyrian conquest: he has ‘devoted to destruction’ the nations and their gods. The rhetorical claim: no god (kol-ʾĕlōhê) could deliver from his hand; why would YHWH be different?
15: wĕʿattāh ʾal-yassî ʾetḵem ḥizkîyāhû wĕʾal-yaddîʾ ʾetḵem kāzōʾt — ‘And now do not let Hezekiah deceive you or mislead you like this.’ The Hiphil nassāh (entice, deceive) and yaddîʾ (from the Hiphil of dāʾāh or possibly yiddaʿ: make know/mislead) emphasize deception. The assertion follows: kî lōʾ-yûkal kol-ʾĕlôah kol-gôy ûmamlāḵāh lĕhaṣṣîl ʿammô miyyāḏî = ‘for no god of any nation or kingdom has been able to deliver his people from my hand.’ The universal claim — kol-ʾĕlôah, any god — sets up the narrative refutation.
16: Sennacherib’s servants continued to speak against YHWH God and against Hezekiah his servant. The addition ʿaḇdô (his servant) to Hezekiah’s description is theologically pointed: Hezekiah is YHWH’s ʿeḇed (servant), the same royal-Davidic title. Blaspheming Hezekiah as servant is an indirect attack on YHWH himself.
17: wĕsēper — and a letter (sēper). The written dispatch (sēper = document, book, letter) from Sennacherib amplifies the spoken threat. The content is described as lĕgaddēp ʿal-YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl = ‘to blaspheme/reproach against YHWH God of Israel.’ gāḏap (to reproach, blaspheme) appears in Numbers 15:30 for the high-handed sinner and Psalms 44:16 for divine reproach. The parallel with Isa 36-37 (Hezekiah lays the letter before YHWH in the temple) suggests the letter was a formal diplomatic-religious challenge.
18: wayyiqrĕʾû bĕqôl gādôl yĕhûdît — ‘They cried with a loud voice in the Judean [language].’ yĕhûdît (Judahite/Hebrew, the language of Judah) is the term used in 2 Kings 18:26, 28 to distinguish the vernacular from Aramaic. The political-psychological strategy: the Assyrian negotiators deliberately chose the people’s own language to undermine civilian morale, bypassing the official negotiators. The phrase lĕharʾît ûlĕbahᵃlām = ‘to frighten and terrify them’ — yārēʾ (to fear) and bāhal (to be disturbed, terrified) — confirms that psychological warfare was the explicit goal.
19: wayyḏabbĕrû ʾel-ʾĕlōhê yĕrûšālayim — ‘They spoke against the God of Jerusalem’ as if YHWH were like the gods of the peoples of the earth (kĕʾĕlōhê ʿammê hāʾāreṣ) — the work of human hands (maʿăśēh yĕḏê hāʾāḏām). The equation of YHWH with handmade gods is the core blasphemy. The Chronicler’s formulation is precise: the Assyrians argued by category error — grouping YHWH with manufactured gods. The polemic against ‘works of human hands’ appears throughout the prophets (Isaiah 2:8; Jeremiah 10:3-5; Psalms 115:4-7) as the definitional weakness of idols.
20: wayyitpallēl ḥizkîyāhû hammelek ûyĕšaʿyāhû ben-ʾāmôṣ hannāḇîʾ ʿal-zōʾt wayyizʿaqû haššāmāyim — ‘King Hezekiah and Isaiah son of Amoz the prophet prayed about this and cried to heaven.’ The dual subject prayer (king + prophet) is unique in the historical books. zāʿaq haššāmāyim (cried to the heavens) is the direct address of prayer; the spatial directional (haššāmāyim = the heavens) frames YHWH as enthroned above, accessible by upward-directed speech. The combination of hitpallēl (formal intercessory prayer) and zāʿaq (urgent cry) signals both structured petition and emotional urgency.
21: wayyi$laḥ YHWH malʾāk — ‘YHWH sent an angel.’ The identity of the agent is minimally specified: simply malʾāk (messenger/angel). The Chronicler does not cite the 185,000 figure from 2 Kings 19:35 but describes the outcome as: wayyaṣmēt kol-gibbôr ḥayil wĕnāgîḏ wĕśār = ‘it destroyed every mighty warrior, leader, and officer.’ The three-fold military hierarchy (gibbôr / nāgîḏ / śar) encompasses the entire command structure. wayyāšāḇ ḇōšet pānîm lĕʾarṣô = Sennacherib ‘returned in shame of face to his land’ — bōšet pānîm (shame of face) is public disgrace that reverses the honor of conquest.
22: wayyôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-ḥizkîyāhû wĕʾēt yôšĕḇê yĕrûšālayim miyyaḏ sannḥērîḇ melek ʾaššûr ûmiyyaḏ-kōl — ‘YHWH saved Hezekiah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem from the hand of Sennacherib.’ The Hiphil wāyôšaʿ (he saved, from yāšaʿ) is the Exodus/salvation vocabulary applied to the deliverance from Assyria. wayyĕnahᵃlēm miSSāḇîḇ = ‘he gave them rest on every side’ (nāḥāl in Piel: to guide, give inheritance/rest; miSSāḇîḇ = from all around). The rest-on-every-side formula recalls the Davidic promise (1 Chronicles 22:9) and Deuteronomic blessing (Deuteronomy 12:10).
23: wĕraббîm mĕḇîʾîm minḥāh laYHWH lîrûšālāyim — ‘Many brought gifts to YHWH in Jerusalem.’ The tribute-bringing in the aftermath of deliverance echoes the Solomonic scenario (2 Chronicles 9): nations coming to Jerusalem with gifts. The nations (wĕladōnîm laḵol-haggôyim) who see the deliverance bring tribute and valuable gifts to Hezekiah. The reward of international recognition is the Chronicler’s indicator that YHWH’s vindication had global visibility.
24: bāyyāmîm hāhēm ḥālāh ḥizkîyāhû ʿaḏ-lāmût — ‘In those days Hezekiah was sick to the point of death.’ The timing ‘in those days’ is the same phrase used in 2 Kings 20:1 and Isaiah 38:1, placing the illness in the same period as the Sennacherib crisis. wayyitpallēl ʾel-YHWH = he prayed to YHWH. wayyōʾmer lô wĕʾôt nātan lô = ‘he spoke to him and gave him a sign (ʾôt).’ The sign (the shadow going back on the sundial) is referenced but not described; the Chronicler presupposes reader knowledge from the Isaiah 38 parallel. The ʾôt (sign) as divine authentication of prophetic promise is standard (Exodus 4:8-9; Isaiah 7:11-14).
25: kî-hē ḵiḇ libbô wayyigbah libbô — ‘but Hezekiah’s heart was proud.’ The idiom gāḇah lēḇ (to lift/elevate the heart = pride) is exactly the same formula used of Uzziah in 2 Chronicles 26:16. The term kî-hē ḵiḇ (reciprocal: ‘not corresponding to the benefit done to him’) signals the ingratitude framework: Hezekiah failed to respond in kind (kĕgāmlô = according to/corresponding to the good done to him). The wrath (qeṣep) came upon him and upon Judah and Jerusalem. This brief noticing of pride — absent from 2 Kings — is characteristically Chronicler: even the best kings are susceptible to the prosperity-to-pride pattern.
26: wayyiḵkānēʿ ḥizkîyāhû bĕgōḇah libbô — ‘Hezekiah humbled himself in the pride of his heart.’ kānaʿ (Niphal: to be humbled, brought low) is the Chronicler’s key repentance verb (cf. 2 Chronicles 12:6-7, 12; 2 Chronicles 33:12, 19, 23; 2 Chronicles 34:27; 2 Chronicles 36:12). The preposition bĕgōḇah libbô (in/because of the height of his heart) makes the object of the humbling explicit: he humbled himself precisely in the thing that was exalted. wattiškoḵ ʿālāyw qeṣep YHWH = ‘the wrath of YHWH did not come upon him in the days of Hezekiah.’ The averted judgment through humbling is the Chronicler’s reward of repentance.
27: The description of Hezekiah’s wealth and glory (kāḇôḏ ʿōšer) follows the pattern of Solomon’s wealth catalog (2 Chronicles 9). ʿāśāh-lô ʾōṣĕrôt = he made storehouses (ʾôṣār) for silver, gold, precious stones, spices, shields, and all desirable things. The list mimics the Solomonic tribute inventory, implying that Hezekiah’s reign represents a second peak of covenant prosperity after the Solomonic golden age.
28: ûmiškenôt lĕtĕḇûʾat dāgān wĕtîrôš wĕyiṣhār — storehouses for grain, wine, and oil. The agricultural triad (grain, wine, oil) is the Deuteronomic prosperity formula (Deuteronomy 7:13; 2 Chronicles 11:14; 2 Chronicles 14:23), the covenant blessings of the land. Their appearance in Hezekiah’s catalog signals covenant-blessing fulfillment. rĕpātôt lĕkol-bĕhēmōt = stalls for all kinds of livestock; wĕʿadrôt laṣṣōʾn = sheepfolds. The specificity signals real administrative infrastructure, not just rhetorical prosperity.
29: ûʿārîm bānāh lô — and cities he built for himself. The building of cities (ʿārîm) is the administrative-expansion indicator of a successful reign (cf. Solomon in 2 Chronicles 8:4-6; Jehoshaphat in 2 Chronicles 17:12). ûmikneh-ṣōʾn ûḇāqār lārōḇ = herds of sheep and cattle in abundance. kî nātan-lô ʾĕlōhîm rĕkûš rāḇ mĕʾōḏ = ‘for God gave him very great possessions.’ The Chronicler’s closing attribution: all the wealth is divine gift (nātan), not human accumulation.
30: wayyiśkōr ḥizkîyāhû ʾet-môṣāʾ-mêyê gîḥôn haʿelyônāh — ‘Hezekiah blocked the upper outlet of the Gihon spring.’ This verse refers to the Siloam tunnel (Hezekiah’s tunnel), one of the best-confirmed archaeological remains of the Israelite monarchy period. The 533-meter tunnel, discovered in 1880 with its inscription intact, was cut through bedrock to divert the Gihon spring inside Jerusalem’s walls. wayyayyᵉšārēm lĕmaṭṭāh māʿǎrab = ‘and directed them straight down to the west side of the city of David.’ The Chronicler summarizes the hydrological engineering in one verse; 2 Kings 20:20 also references it without description. wayyaṣlaḥ ḥizkîyāhû = Hezekiah prospered.
31: ûḇên haSSîrîm hammalʾāḵîm śarê ḇāḇel — ‘in the matter of the envoys of the princes of Babylon.’ The Babylonian diplomatic mission (also in 2 Kings 20:12-15 and Isa 39) is noted with a theological comment absent from Kings: ʿăzāḇô hāʾĕlōhîm lĕnassōtô lāḏaʿat kol-bĕlibbô = ‘God left him to himself in order to test him, to know all that was in his heart.’ nāsāh (to test, prove) is the divine testing verb of Genesis 22:1 and Exodus 15:25; 2 Chronicles 16:4; 2 Chronicles 20:20. The Chronicler’s editorial comment is unique: YHWH deliberately withdrew support to reveal what was truly in Hezekiah’s heart. This explains his pride (v25): the Babylonian embassy was the test-context.
32: wĕyeter diḇrê ḥizkîyāhû waḥăsāḏāyw hinnām kĕtûḇîm — ‘The rest of the acts of Hezekiah and his good deeds, they are written.’ ḥăsāḏāyw (his acts of faithfulness/loyal love) — ḥeseḏ applied to a king’s acts is the covenant-loyalty vocabulary. The Chronicler cites the vision of Isaiah son of Amoz the prophet (ḥăzôn yĕšaʿyāhû ben-ʾāmôṣ hannāḇîʾ) in the book of the kings of Judah and Israel as the primary source. Citing a prophetic vision (ḥāzôn) as the historical source signals that Hezekiah’s reign was prophetically witnessed and interpreted, not merely chronicled.
33: wayyiškab ḥizkîyāhû ʿim-ʾăḇôtāyw — ‘Hezekiah slept with his fathers.’ The standard regnal death formula. wayyiqbĕrûhû bĕmaʿălēh qiḇrê ḇĕnê-ḏāwîḏ = buried in the ascent of the tombs of the sons of David. The distinction from ‘in the city of David’ (the more honorific burial ground) is preserved; yet the Chronicler adds wĕḵāḇôḏ nātĕnû-lô ḇĕmôtô kol-yĕhûḏāh wĕyôšĕḇê yĕrûšālāyim = ‘all Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem honored him at his death.’ The community’s honor at burial (kāḇôḏ) is the social validation of a good reign.
